steps.title: What Do You Do?
steps.desc: Job words — and every single one is a Sino-Korean compound. You'll see the SAME building blocks from Unit 1 reappearing. This is the LEGO system in action.
goals: See 학, 생, 원, 사 reused across words

steps.note:
SINO-KOREAN: 학 (study) + 생 (life, person).
'Study-person' = student.
학 reappears in: 학교, 학원, 대학교...
생 reappears in: 선생님, 생일, 생활...

COMPOUND: 선생 (teacher) + 님 (honorific).
님 shows RESPECT. You add it to titles.
선생 alone is informal. 님 elevates it.
님 reappears in: 사장님, 부장님, 의사 선생님...

SINO-KOREAN: 의 (medicine) + 사 (master/expert).
의 reappears in: 의학 (medicine), 의원 (clinic).
사 (master/expert) reappears in: 교사, 강사...

SINO-KOREAN: 회사 (company) + 원 (member).
'Company-member' = office worker.
원 reappears in: 병원, 학원, 공무원...

SINO-KOREAN: 경찰 (police) + 관 (official).
관: same building block as 도서관!
경 (warn) + 찰 (observe): police.

SINO-KOREAN: 요리 (cooking) + 사 (expert).
Same 사 as 의사!
요리: cooking. Also a verb: 요리하다 (to cook).

SINO-KOREAN: 간호 (nursing/care) + 사 (expert).
Same 사 (expert) three times: 의사, 요리사, 간호사.
사 = professional expert in a field.

KONGLISH: English 'freelancer' → 프리랜서.
Korea has many freelancers (프리랜서).
Note: 예요 not 이에요 (ends in vowel sound).

steps.title: 🧱 The 사 (師/士) Morpheme: Expert in a Field
steps.text:
You've now seen 사 (expert) THREE times:

의사 (의+사)
→ doctor (medicine + expert)

요리사 (요리+사)
→ chef (cooking + expert)

간호사 (간호+사)
→ nurse (nursing + expert)

And 원 (member) twice:
회사원
→ office worker (company + member)

병원 (coming soon)
→ hospital (illness + place)

8 morphemes → already 10+ words decoded.
Every lesson, your LEGO set gets bigger.

steps.q: 학생 분해: 학 + 생. 학 means:
의사, 요리사, 간호사 — what do they all share?
Which word uses 님 to add RESPECT to a title?
steps.hint: 님 is an honorific suffix added to titles
요리사 = chef, and it ends in a vowel → 예요
Expert morpheme used in 의사, 요리사, 간호사
It sounds like the English word

steps.title: There Is / There Isn't
steps.desc: 있다 does three jobs in one: 'there is', 'I have', and 'it exists'. Korean sees these as the SAME idea. This lesson also introduces 이/가 naturally — just notice it, don't stress it.
goals: Use 있어요 for 'there is' AND 'I have'
Notice 이/가 landing naturally on subjects

steps.exampleEn: There is coffee. / I have coffee.
steps.note:
있다: to exist / to have.
있어요: polite present form.

Works for BOTH 'there is' and 'I have':
커피가 있어요: There is coffee.
저는 고양이가 있어요: I have a cat.

Korean doesn't always separate existence from possession.

steps.exampleEn: There is no time. / I don't have time.
steps.note:
없다 = to not exist / to not have.
없어요 = polite present form.

있어요
→ there is / I have

없어요
→ there is not / I don't have

Exact opposites. Pair them in your memory.

You'll see 이/가 on subjects.

이 after consonant: 시간이 (time)
가 after vowel: 커피가 (coffee)

Just notice the pattern for now.
Don't memorize a rule yet.
Unit 5 will give this a proper name.

steps.title: 💡 있다: Korean's Most Useful Verb
steps.text:
있다 covers THREE English ideas:

1. There is:
커피가 있어요.
→ There is coffee.

2. I have:
저는 차가 있어요.
→ I have a car.

3. Someone is somewhere:
선생님이 교실에 있어요.
→ The teacher is in the classroom.

없다 is the exact inverse of all three:

커피가 없어요.
→ There is no coffee. / I don't have coffee.

One verb doing the work of three English verbs.

opts: There is coffee / I have coffee
steps.ans: There is coffee / I have coffee
steps.hint: 있어요 covers both 'there is' and 'I have'

steps.q: 시간이 없어요. Which particle is on 시간?

steps.hint: 커피 ends in a vowel → 가
Don't have = the opposite of 있어요

steps.q: 'There is no doctor' — choose the correct sentence:

steps.hint: 있어요 = have, when used with a possessed object
Just notice for now — Unit 5 names this properly

steps.title: The Rule Behind Every Verb Ending
steps.desc: In Unit 1 you noticed: 가요 vs 먹어요. Why different endings? There's a single rule that governs this. You've been following it correctly without knowing it. Now you'll know why.
goals: Generate ANY regular verb form from the stem alone

steps.title: ☀️ Bright Vowels Take -아요
steps.text:
Korean splits vowels into two families.

☀️ BRIGHT vowels: ㅏ and ㅗ
These attract the ending -아요.

Look at the LAST VOWEL of the stem.
If it's ㅏ or ㅗ, the verb takes -아요.

가다: stem 가, vowel ㅏ (bright) → -아요
오다: stem 오, vowel ㅗ (bright) → -아요
보다: stem 보, vowel ㅗ (bright) → -아요

Two bright sounds prefer each other.

steps.note:
가다: stem 가.
Vowel ㅏ is bright.
가 + 아요 → ㅏ+ㅏ same vowel merges.
가요.

오다: stem 오.
Vowel ㅗ is bright.
오 + 아요 → ㅗ+ㅏ contracts to ㅘ.
와요.

보다: stem 보.
Vowel ㅗ is bright.
보 + 아요 → ㅗ+ㅏ contracts to ㅘ.
봐요.

Same contraction as 오다.

steps.title: 🌙 All Other Vowels Take -어요
steps.text:
Every vowel that is NOT ㅏ or ㅗ is dark.
Dark vowels take -어요.

ㅓ, ㅜ, ㅡ, ㅣ, ㅔ, ㅐ and all others.

먹다: stem 먹, vowel ㅓ (dark) → -어요
마시다: stem 마시, last vowel ㅣ (dark) → -어요
있다: stem 있, vowel ㅣ (dark) → -어요

Bright = only ㅏ and ㅗ.
Dark = literally everything else.

steps.note:
먹다: stem 먹.
Vowel ㅓ is dark.
먹 + 어요 = 먹어요.

Consonant stems: the ending just attaches.

마시다: stem 마시.
Last vowel ㅣ is dark.
마시 + 어요 → ㅣ+ㅓ contracts to ㅕ.
마셔요.

steps.title: ✨ 하다 Is Special
steps.text:
하다 follows bright rules (ㅏ is bright).
But it contracts tightly:

하 + 아요 → 하아요? No.
하 + 아 contracts to 해.
해 + 요 = 해요.

하다 → ALWAYS 해요. No exceptions.

This means ALL 하다 verbs work the same:
공부하다 → 공부해요
운동하다 → 운동해요
요리하다 → 요리해요

steps.q: 배우다 (to learn) — stem vowel is ㅜ. Which ending?
steps.hint: ㅜ is dark (not ㅏ or ㅗ) → -어요. 배우 + 어요 → 배워요 (ㅜ+ㅓ contracts)

steps.q: 오다 (to come) — stem vowel is ㅗ. Which ending?
steps.hint: ㅗ is BRIGHT → -아요. 오 + 아요 → 와요 (contraction)
읽다: stem 읽, last vowel is ㅣ. ㅣ is dark → -어요 → 읽어요

steps.s: 살다 (to live) — stem 살, vowel ㅏ → ___
steps.hint: ㅏ is BRIGHT → -아요. 살 + 아요 = 살아요

steps.s: 서다 (to stand) — stem 서, vowel ㅓ → ___
steps.hint: ㅓ is dark → -어요. 서 + 어요 = 서요 (ㅓ + ㅓ contracts)

steps.title: 🔗 Contraction Patterns
steps.text:
When the stem vowel meets the ending vowel, they merge:

① Same vowel merges: ㅏ+ㅏ → ㅏ
가 + 아요 = 가요

② ㅗ+ㅏ → ㅘ
오 + 아요 = 와요
보 + 아요 = 봐요

③ ㅣ+ㅓ → ㅕ
마시 + 어요 = 마셔요

④ ㅡ drops entirely:
쓰다 → 쓰 + 어요 = 써요

Don't memorise these.
Just SAY the contraction — your mouth prefers the shorter form.

steps.hint: 이 + 어 contracts to 여. 마시 + 어요 = 마셔요
ㅗ + ㅏ contracts to ㅘ. 보 + 아요 = 봐요

steps.title: 🎯 You Can Now Generate ANY Regular Verb
steps.text:
Test yourself. You've never seen this verb:

앉다 = to sit
Stem: 앉
Last vowel: ㅏ (bright)
Ending: -아요
앉 + 아요 = 앉아요 ✓

Or this one:
쓰다 = to write
Stem: 쓰
Vowel: ㅡ (dark, and ㅡ drops)
쓰 + 어요 = 써요 ✓

You derived these yourself.
You did not memorise them.
That is the difference between a phrase learner and a language learner.

steps.hint: Bright stem, dark stem, dark contracted

steps.q: You see a new verb: 놀다 (to play). Stem 놀, vowel ㅗ. Polite form?
steps.hint: ㅗ is BRIGHT → -아요. 놀 + 아요 = 놀아요
하 + 아 always contracts to 해. 해요. No exceptions.

steps.title: This / That / That Over There
steps.desc: Korean sees space in THREE levels where English sees two. The 이/그/저 prefix runs through everything: things, places, directions. Master the prefix — master the system.
goals: See how it connects to 여기/거기/저기

steps.note:
이: near ME (the speaker).
것: thing.
이것: this thing.

이 prefix rule: near the SPEAKER.

그: near YOU (the listener).
그것: that thing near you.

Also used for things just mentioned:
'그 사람' = 'that person' (already mentioned).

저: far from BOTH of us.
저것: that thing way over there.

The full three-level system:
이: near ME | 그: near YOU | 저: far from BOTH.

steps.example: 이 가방 / 그 책 / 저 건물
steps.exampleEn: This bag / that book / that building over there
steps.note:
The prefix drops 것 and attaches to a noun:
이것 → 이 [noun]: 이 가방 (this bag)
그것 → 그 [noun]: 그 책 (that book)
저것 → 저 [noun]: 저 건물 (that building)

Same 이/그/저 logic, just + a specific noun.

steps.title: 🔺 One Prefix, Three Systems
steps.text:
이/그/저 is ONE pattern running through everything:

이것/그것/저것 → things
여기/거기/저기 → places (next lesson!)
이쪽/그쪽/저쪽 → directions
이분/그분/저분 → people (honorific)

Learn the prefix ONCE.
Get four systems for free.

English: this ↔ that (2 levels)
Korean: 이 ↔ 그 ↔ 저 (3 levels)

steps.q: Something is right next to YOU (not me). You call it:
A mountain far from both of us. You say:
'That book (near you)' as a short form:
steps.hint: Drop 것, add the noun directly

steps.s: ___ 건물이 아름다워요. (That building over there is beautiful)
이것은 제 가방이에요. {1}은 선생님 가방이에요. {2}은 저기 있어요.
steps.hint: My bag, teacher's bag (near them), the third one is far

steps.q: 그것 is used when the object is:
Which prefix system do 이것/그것/저것 share with 여기/거기/저기?
steps.hint: The same distance-based prefix runs through both

steps.note:
보다: to see, to watch, to look.

One of the 5 most common Korean verbs.
보다 → 봐요 (polite present: ㅗ+ㅏ = ㅘ contraction).

영화를 봐요: I watch a movie.
텔레비전을 봐요: I watch TV.
이거 봐요!: Look at this!

보다 appears EVERYWHERE in Korean.
You'll use it every day.

말하다: to speak, to say.
말 (speech/words) + 하다 (do).

말하다 → 말해요 (하다 verb: always 해요).

한국어로 말해요: I speak in Korean.
영어로 말해요: I speak in English.
천천히 말해 주세요: Please speak slowly.

-(으)로 말하다: speak IN [language].

steps.hint: 보: ㅗ+ㅏ contracts to ㅘ. 봐요.
말하다 = to speak. 한국어로 = in Korean.

steps.title: Here / There / Over There
steps.desc: Same three-way logic, now for PLACES. And a bonus: 저기요! is how you get a waiter's attention — which you'll use on day one in Korea.
goals: Use 여기, 거기, 저기 for places
Connect this system back to 이/그/저

steps.note:
여기: here, near the speaker.
Same 이 logic: near ME.

여기요!: 'Here!' (used to direct someone here).
여기 있어요: 'It's here.'

거기: there, near the listener.
Same 그 logic: near YOU.

거기 있어요?: 'Is it there (by you)?'

저기: over there, far from both.
Same 저 logic: far from BOTH.

저기요!: 'Excuse me!' — to get someone's attention.
Literally: 'Hey, over there!' Used for waiters, strangers.
One of Korea's most useful words.

steps.en: this way / that way / that way (far)
steps.note:
쪽: direction/side.
이쪽: this direction (toward me).
그쪽: that direction (toward you).
저쪽: that direction (far).

이쪽으로: this way → (으)로 = direction particle (Unit 6).

steps.title: 📍 저기요: The Word That Gets You Fed
steps.text:
The most useful single word for visiting Korea:

저기요!
→ Excuse me! (literally: hey, over there!)

여기요!
→ Hey, over here! (also used, slightly less common)

저기요 works for:
• Calling a waiter in a restaurant
• Getting a stranger's attention
• Flagging down a taxi
• Alerting a shopkeeper

저기요 is the safe default.

steps.q: To call a waiter in a Korean restaurant:
steps.hint: 저기요 = Excuse me! (to get attention from afar)

steps.q: You want your friend (standing near you) to come toward you. You say:
거기 maps to which 이/그/저 prefix?
steps.hint: 거기 = there, near YOU = 그 logic

steps.q: 화장실이 어디 있어요? You look and say '저기요!' Wait — what does 저기요 mean here?

steps.s: 지하철역이 ___에 있어요. (The subway station is over there)

steps.hint: Standard way to call a waiter

steps.s: A: {1}요! 메뉴 주세요. B: 네, {2} 있어요.
steps.hint: Call the waiter, then: the menu is right here

opts: They all use the 이/그/저 distance prefix
steps.ans: They all use the 이/그/저 distance prefix

steps.hint: 이쪽 = this direction (toward speaker), 오세요 = please come

steps.title: Meet the Family (Preview)
steps.desc: A first look at family vocabulary. The big reveal: which word you use for 'older sibling' depends on YOUR gender, not the sibling's. Korean speakers encode speaker identity into their vocabulary.
goals: Prepare for the full family unit (U5)

steps.note:
엄마: casual (mom).
어머니: formal (mother).

Kids and adults alike use 엄마 with their own mother.
Use 어머니 about other people's mothers.
Note: 우리 엄마 = 'our mom' = Korean says 'our', not 'my'.

아빠: casual (dad).
아버지: formal (father).

The double ㅃ in 아빠 is a tense consonant.
Audio tip: it sounds clipped and forceful.

steps.exampleEn: I have an older brother. (said by a female)
steps.note:
TWO words. ONE meaning. Different speakers:
형: used by MALE speakers.
오빠: used by FEMALE speakers.

The SPEAKER's gender determines the word.
Not the sibling's gender.

steps.exampleEn: I have an older sister. (said by a male)
steps.note:
Same rule as 형/오빠:
누나: used by MALE speakers.
언니: used by FEMALE speakers.

Full grid:
Male speaker: 형 (older bro), 누나 (older sis)
Female speaker: 오빠 (older bro), 언니 (older sis)

동생: younger sibling.
남동생: younger BROTHER (남: male).
여동생: younger SISTER (여: female).

Good news: no speaker-gender split for younger siblings.
Difference only for OLDER siblings.

steps.title: 🎯 Korean Siblings: It's About YOU, Not Them
steps.text:
In English: one word per sibling type.
'Older brother' = older brother, done.

In Korean: THE SPEAKER'S GENDER MATTERS:

[YOU are MALE]
형 = my older brother
누나 = my older sister

[YOU are FEMALE]
오빠 = my older brother
언니 = my older sister

These words also extend OUTSIDE family:
오빠: how female fans call male idols!
언니: how Korean women address older female friends.
형: how Korean men address older male friends.

They're relationship words, not just family labels.

steps.q: A female speaker says '오빠가 있어요.' She has:
steps.hint: 오빠 = older brother, used by FEMALE speakers

steps.q: A male speaker wants to say 'I have an older sister':
Which sibling term does NOT change based on speaker gender?
steps.hint: Younger sibling = 동생 regardless of speaker
오빠 ends in vowel → subject marker 가
Female speaker: older brother + older sister

steps.q: Outside the family, 오빠 is used by:
opts: Female speakers for older males they're close to
steps.ans: Female speakers for older males they're close to
steps.hint: 오빠 extends to friendships and fandoms

steps.q: 'I have a younger brother' in Korean:

steps.title: Unlock Words With Building Blocks
steps.desc: ~60% of Korean vocabulary comes from Chinese characters. Every morpheme you learn today unlocks 5-15 future words. This session is an investment that pays forever.
goals: Build your LEGO morpheme bank to 10+ pieces

steps.note:
SINO-KOREAN: 학 (study) + 교 (teach).
'Study-teach place': school.

학: same as 학생!
교 reappears in: 교실 (classroom), 교육 (education), 교사 (teacher).

SINO-KOREAN: 도서 (books) + 관 (building).
'Book-building' = library.

관 reappears in: 영화관 (cinema), 미술관 (art museum), 박물관 (museum), 경찰관 (police officer)!

SINO-KOREAN: 교 (teach) + 실 (room).
'Teach-room' = classroom.

실 reappears in: 사무실 (office), 회의실 (meeting room), 화장실 (bathroom).

SINO-KOREAN: 병 (illness) + 원 (place/institution).
'Illness-place' = hospital.

원: same as 회사원! But here 원 means 'institution/place'.
원 appears as BOTH 'member' and 'place' — context tells you which.

SINO-KOREAN: 약 (medicine) + 국 (bureau/bureau).
'Medicine-place' = pharmacy.

약: medicine. Reappears in 약사 (pharmacist = medicine + expert!).

steps.title: 🏗️ Your Morpheme Bank So Far
steps.text:
You now know these Sino-Korean building blocks:

학 (study): 학생, 학교, 학원...
생 (life/person): 학생, 선생님, 생일...
교 (teach): 학교, 교실, 교육...
관 (building): 도서관, 영화관, 미술관...
실 (room): 교실, 사무실, 화장실...
원 (member/place): 회사원, 병원, 학원...
의 (medicine): 의사, 의학...
사 (expert): 의사, 요리사, 간호사...
약 (medicine): 약국, 약사...
병 (illness): 병원, 병...

10 morphemes → already 20+ words decoded.
By Unit 14: ~80 morphemes → 500+ words.

steps.q: 교실 and 화장실 both share 실. 실 means:

steps.s: ___에 가요. 약을 사요. (Going to get medicine)
교___이 어디에 있어요? (Where is the classroom?)

steps.hint: Study at school, books are at the library

steps.title: Also / Too
steps.desc: One small particle with big frequency. 도 REPLACES 은/는 and 이/가 — it never stacks onto them. Master this rule now and avoid the most common beginner particle error.
goals: Understand that 도 replaces other particles
Use 저도요 as an instant reply

steps.note:
도: also / too.

도 REPLACES 은/는 or 이/가:
저는 학생이에요 → 저도 학생이에요.
커피가 있어요 → 커피도 있어요.

도 NEVER stacks:
저는도 ✗ / 커피가도 ✗
Just: 저도 ✓ / 커피도 ✓

steps.exampleEn: A: I'm a student. B: Me too!
steps.note:
저도요: 'Me too!' Quick, natural reply.

저도요 = 저 (I) + 도 (also) + 요 (polite).

One of the most common expressions in Korean conversation.

steps.exampleEn: There is also coffee. / I also have coffee.
steps.note:
도 replaces 이/가 on the subject too:
커피가 있어요 → 커피도 있어요 (also have coffee)
시간이 없어요 → 시간도 없어요 (also no time)

Negative 도: '도 없어요' = 'also don't have'

도 + negative = 'neither' / 'don't ... either':
저도 몰라요: I don't know either.
저도 없어요: I don't have it either.
저도 학생이 아니에요: I'm not a student either.

Same particle, different meaning with negatives.

steps.title: ⚡ 도 Replaces — Never Stacks
steps.text:
도 REPLACES 은/는 / 이/가. It never stacks.

✗ 저는도 학생이에요 (WRONG — can't stack)
✗ 커피가도 있어요 (WRONG — can't stack)

✓ 저도 학생이에요.
→ I am also a student.

✓ 커피도 있어요.
→ There is coffee too.

✓ 선생님도 있어요.
→ The teacher is also here.

도 = swap, not addition.

steps.q: Your friend says 저는 커피가 좋아요. You agree. Your reply:

steps.hint: 도 replaces 가 — never stacks with it

steps.s: 커피___ 있어요. 차도 있어요. (There is ALSO coffee)
A: 저는 배가 고파요. B: 저___ 배가 고파요!
선생님___ 없어요. (The teacher isn't here either)
저는 학생이에요. 제 친구{1} 학생이에요. 우리 선생님{2} 여기 있어요.
steps.hint: Friend is ALSO a student. Teacher is ALSO here.

steps.title: Where Things Are
steps.desc: 에 is one of Korean's most important particles. Today: one job only — marking WHERE something is. You'll see more of 에 in Unit 6. For now, use it for location.
goals: Use [place]에 있어요 to say where things are
Get comfortable with 에 as a location particle

steps.note:
에 = marks WHERE something IS (static location).

학교에 있어요.
→ (I'm) at school.

카페에 있어요.
→ (I'm) at the café.

집에 있어요.
→ (I'm) at home.

Full 에 system taught in Unit 6.
Pattern: [place]에 있어요 = is/am at [place].

어디: where.
어디에 있어요?: 'Where is it?'

어디에 있어요 = survival Korean.
The most important question you'll ask in Korea.

집: home/house.
집에 있어요: 'I'm at home' / 'It's at home'.

Native Korean word (not Sino-Korean).
집에 가요: 'going home' (에 as destination — preview of U6!)

KONGLISH: English 'café' → 카페.
카페에 있어요: 'I'm at the café.'

Korea has more cafés per capita than any country.
카페 culture is massive here.

지금: now.
지금 어디에 있어요?: 'Where are you right now?'

SINO-KOREAN: 지 (this) + 금 (now).
Very common time word.

steps.q: 지금 어디에 있어요? Someone is asking:
steps.hint: 지금 = now, 어디에 있어요 = where is
At home = 집 + location particle + 있어요

steps.s: A: 지금 {1}에 있어요? B: 아니요, {2}에 있어요.
steps.hint: Are you at school? No, I'm at the café.

steps.q: 학교에 있어요. The 에 particle marks:

steps.hint: It comes from the English/French word café

steps.title: I Know / I Don't Know
steps.desc: Two essential expressions. And an honest heads-up: 모르다 does something unexpected when conjugated. We'll flag it and move on — the explanation comes in Unit 13.

steps.note:
알다: to know.
알아요: polite present form.

알아요: 'I know (this information).'
이해해요: 'I understand (a concept)' — but 알아요 covers both in daily use.

모르다 = to not know.

⚡ JRTF — Just Remember This Form:

모르다 → 몰라요
→ don't know (NOT 모르어요 — the stem transforms)

Irregular verb. Full pattern taught in Unit 13.
For now: 몰라요 is a set phrase.

맞다: to be correct/right.
맞아요: polite form. 'That's correct.'

맞아요 is used to confirm someone is right.
틀려요: 'That's wrong.' (opposite)

Very common in conversation.

steps.exampleEn: A: I'm a doctor. B: Really?
steps.note:
정말: really / truly.
정말요?: 'Really?' (surprised/interested reaction)

정말요 = 정말 (really) + 요 (polite).

진짜요?: same meaning, more casual.
(진짜 = real/true, more emphatic than 정말)

steps.title: ⚡ JRTF: Just Remember This Form
steps.text:
You'll occasionally meet verbs that change shape when conjugated.

모르다 → 몰라요 (expected: 모르어요 ✗)

This is called an IRREGULAR verb.
Don't try to figure out the pattern yet.
The specific rule is taught in Unit 13 (르-irregular).

When you see ⚡ JRTF:
→ Just memorize the given form
→ The explanation is coming later
→ It's not an error, it's a known pattern

For now: 몰라요. Done.

steps.hint: JRTF: Just Remember This Form — 모르다 → 몰라요

steps.q: Why does 모르다 become 몰라요 (not 모르어요)?
opts: It's irregular — pattern taught in Unit 13
steps.ans: It's irregular — pattern taught in Unit 13
steps.hint: JRTF: the pattern has a name, it's just coming later
정말 = really — used when surprised or wanting to confirm

steps.s: A: 선생님이 학교에 있어요. B: 네, ___요. (That's right)

steps.hint: JRTF: 모르다 → the irregular polite form

steps.s: A: 선생님이 어디에 있어요? B: {1}. 저도 {2}.
steps.hint: A asks where the teacher is. B has no idea. Neither does C.
진짜 = real/true, often used with stronger feeling
알아요 is used for both in daily conversation

steps.title: Unit 2 Complete
steps.desc: Testing everything: jobs, existence, demonstratives, family, place, morphemes. Every question uses only vocabulary from Units 1-2.

steps.example: 저는 학생이에요. 학교에 있어요. 친구도 있어요.
steps.exampleEn: I am a student. I'm at school. I also have a friend.
steps.note:
VOCABULARY: 8 jobs, 5 locations, 5 family terms.
GRAMMAR SHOWN (not yet named):
이/가 subject marker
에 location particle
도 'also' particle
있다/없다 existence/possession
DECOMPOSITION: 10 Sino-Korean morphemes banked.

steps.q: Something far from both speaker and listener is called:
관 appears in 도서관 AND 경찰관. It means:
steps.hint: 관 = building/official hall in both words

steps.q: A female speaker saying 오빠가 있어요 has:
steps.hint: 오빠 = older brother, used by female speakers

steps.s: 저는 ___. 그 단어를 몰라요. (I don't know)
steps.hint: JRTF: the irregular form of 모르다

steps.s: 커피도 있어요. 차___ 있어요. (Tea is ALSO there)

steps.hint: I'm at school. A friend is also here.

steps.q: 실 appears in 교실 and 화장실. It means:
